items.title: Atlanta 2012 Qualifying
items.contentHtml:
Atlanta Classification 2012

  • Defines acute pancreatitis as pancreatic inflammation with abdominal pain, amylase/lipase >3 times the upper limit of normal and compatible imaging findings.
  • Two phases: early (≤1 week) with systemic inflammatory response syndrome and organ failure; late (>1 week) with local complications.
  • Severity: mild (no organ failure, no necrosis), moderately severe (transient organ failure ≤48 h or local complications) and severe (persistent organ failure >48 h).
  • Classify collections: peripancreatic fluid collection acute (<4 weeks, without wall) vs pseudocyst (>4 weeks, with wall); acute pancreatic necrosis (<4 weeks, heterogeneous content) vs encapsulated necrosis (>4 weeks).

items.title: Ranson criteria
items.contentHtml:
Ranson criteria

  • Eleven variables: 5 at admission and 6 at 48 hours. On admission: age >55 years, leukocytes >16,000/mm³, glucose >200 mg/dL, LDH >350 IU/L, AST >250 IU/L.
  • At 48 h: hematocrit drops >10%, BUN rises >5 mg/dL, calcium <8 mg/dL, PaO₂ <60 mmHg, base deficit >4 mEq/L, fluid sequestration >6 L.
  • Severity: 0-2 points = mild (mortality <1%); 3-4 = moderate (mortality ~15%); 5-6 = severe (mortality ~40%); ≥7 = very severe (mortality >90%).
  • Cat: Ranson criteria are not used for continuous guidance; They are retrospective (48 hours). Prefer APACHE II or BISAP for early prediction.

items.title: APACHE II in pancreatitis
items.contentHtml:
APACHE II in pancreatitis

  • APACHE II (Acute Physiology And Chronic Health Evaluation II) scores 12 physiological variables + age + chronic disease.
  • Range 0-71; in pancreatitis, score ≥8 predicts severe pancreatitis with ~70% sensitivity.
  • Advantage: it can be calculated upon admission and daily, unlike Ranson which requires 48 hours.
  • Disadvantage: complexity of calculation; In practice, BISAP (BUN, Impaired mental status, SIRS, Age, Pleural effusion) is used as a faster alternative.
  • For ENARM/MIR: APACHE II ≥8 has ~15-20% mortality; ≥15 mortality >50%.

items.title: Indications for contrast-enhanced CT
items.contentHtml:
Indications for CT with contrast

  • Computed tomography with intravenous contrast not indicated on admission if the diagnosis is clear (risk of nephrotoxicity and radiation).
  • Indications: diagnostic doubt, persistent organ failure (>48 h), suspicion of complications (necrosis, collections), clinical worsening or lack of improvement after 48-72 h of management.
  • In severe pancreatitis it is recommended at 72-96 hours to evaluate necrosis (maximum parenchymal enhancement is achieved after 48 hours of onset).
  • Contraindications: allergy to iodinated contrast, renal failure (GFR <30 mL/min), or mild pancreatitis without suspected complications.

items.title: Abdominal compartment syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Abdomen liquids.
  • Measurement: through urinary catheter (intravesical pressure) as the gold standard.
  • Step management: non-surgical decompression (sedation, evacuation of gastrointestinal contents, drainage of ascites) and if it fails, laparostomy or open fascia.
  • Trap: abdominal compartment syndrome increases mortality up to 50-60% if not recognized.

items.title: Tomographic signs of necrosis
items.contentHtml:
Tomographic signs of necrosis

  • In CT with contrast, pancreatic necrosis is defined as areas of parenchyma that do not enhance (<30 HU) after intravenous contrast.
  • The tomographic severity index (CTSI) combines degree of inflammation (A-E) and percentage of necrosis: 0% (0 pt), <30% (2 pt), 30-50% (4 pt), >50% (6 pt). Maximum score 10.
  • Necrosis >30% is associated with increased risk of infection and mortality.
  • Key finding: pancreatic necrosis can be sterile or infected; the presence of gas in the parenchyma suggests infected necrosis.

items.title: Intravenous fluid treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with intravenous fluids

  • Initial pillar: aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation in the first 24 hours to correct hypovolemia.
  • Ringer's lactate is preferred over normal saline (lower risk of hyperchloremia and metabolic acidosis; reduces systemic inflammation).
  • Dose: initial bolus of 10-20 mL/kg, then 1.5-2 mL/kg/h for 24-48 h, adjusting according to urine output (>0.5 mL/kg/h), BUN, hematocrit, and signs of overload.
  • Trap: excessive resuscitation (>4 L/day) is associated with abdominal compartment syndrome and increased mortality. Use hemodynamic goals.

items.title: Prophylactic antibiotics
items.contentHtml:
Indications for prophylactic antibiotics

  • The routine use of prophylactic antibiotics is not recommended in acute pancreatitis (nor in sterile necrosis) to prevent infection.
  • Recent meta-analyses show that they do not reduce mortality or the need for surgery, but they increase fungal infections and bacterial resistance.
  • Indication of antibiotics only if there is infection documented (positive culture, infected necrosis, cholangitis, pneumonia, bacteremia).
  • If infected necrosis is suspected (fever, leukocytosis, gas on CT), start an empirical antibiotic that penetrates necrosis: imipenem (500 mg IV every 6 hours) or meropenem.

items.title: urgent ERCP
items.contentHtml:
Urgent endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography

  • Indicated in acute biliary pancreatitis with concomitant cholangitis (fever, jaundice, pain, cholestasis) or persistent biliary obstruction (bilirubin >5 mg/dL or dilated common bile duct).
  • It should be performed at early onset. 24-48 hafter admission if there is cholangitis; In the absence of cholangitis, urgent ERCP does not improve prognosis and may aggravate pancreatitis.
  • Procedure: endoscopic sphincterotomy + stone extraction. Sphincterotomy alone (without extraction) reduces recurrence.
  • Trap: in mild biliary pancreatitis without cholangitis, conservative management and elective cholecystectomy during the same admission.

items.title: Autoimmune pancreatitis
items.contentHtml:
Autoimmune pancreatitis type 1 and 2

  • AIP type 1: associated with IgG4-related disease (lymphoplasmacytic infiltration with fibrosis, elevation of serum IgG4). It affects older adults, it may have extrapancreatic manifestations (sclerosing cholangitis, retroperitoneal fibrosis).
  • PAI type 2: without elevation of IgG4, associated with inflammatory bowel disease (especially ulcerative colitis). Histology: epithelioid granulocytes in pancreatic ducts.
  • Image: diffusely enlarged pancreas (sausage-shaped) with late enhancement on CT; sometimes focal mass simulating adenocarcinoma.
  • Treatment: corticosteroids (prednisone 30-40 mg/day for 4 weeks, then decrease). Dramatic response in 2-4 weeks.

items.title: Pancreatic pseudocyst
items.contentHtml:
Pancreatic pseudocyst and drainage

  • Definition: liquid collection with fibrous wall, appearing >4 weeks after acute pancreatitis, with elevated amylase in the contents.
  • Indications for drainage: symptomatic pseudocyst (pain, jaundice, gastric/duodenal obstruction), infected, or >6 cm without resolution spontaneous.
  • Methods: endoscopic drainage (first line: cystogastrostomy/cystoduodenostomy with stent placement), percutaneous drainage (increased risk of fistula) or surgical (open/laparoscopic cystogastrostomy).
  • Trap: do not drain asymptomatic pseudocysts, even large ones, because they can resolve themselves in 6-12 months.

items.title: Infected vs sterile necrosis
items.contentHtml:
Infected vs sterile necrosis

  • Sterile necrosis: conservative management (support, fluids, enteral nutrition). It does not require antibiotics or drainage unless it becomes infected.
  • Infected necrosis: suspect due to fever, leukocytosis, clinical worsening after 7-10 days, or gas on CT. Confirm with CT-guided fine needle aspiration (culture + Gram).
  • Treatment: empirical antibiotic therapy (imipenem or meropenem) and drainage (endoscopic, percutaneous or surgical). Necrosectomy is reserved for cases that do not improve with drainage.
  • Trap: sterile necrosis should not be subjected to necrosectomy; increases mortality. The step-up approach (drainage first, then surgery if it fails) is the standard.
